Facts.text: Greco-Persian Wars: Battle of Mycale is won by Greek forces over Persian naval troops on the Ionian coast
Greco-Persian Wars: Battle of Plataea, Persian forces led by Mardonius are routed by a Greek army under Pausanias; a double victory with the success at Mycale that ends the Persian invasion
Battle of Baekgang: Tang Chinese and Silla Korean forces defeat Baekje Korean forces and their Yamato Japanese allies on the Geum River in Korea, the last Japanese invasion of Korea for 900 years
Japanese legal code Goseibai Shikimoku or the Formulary of Adjudications is promulgated by Regent Hōjō Yasutoki
Battle of Dussindale: John Dudley Earl of Warwick destroys Robert Kett's army, ending Kett's rebellion in Norfolk, England
Pope Pius V names Cosimo I de'Medici, Duke of Florence as Grand Duke of Tuscany
Duke of Parma's troops occupy Antwerp
Olivier van Noort completes first Dutch circumnavigation of the globe, returning home after two years but with little success at trading or taking Spanish possessions
Polish King Wladyslaw IV Vasa claims crown as King of Russia (disputed)
German Frederick Elector of the Palatinate is chosen as King of Bohemia
Battle of Lutter: Catholic League defeats Danish King Christian IV
Java: Sultan Agung of Mataram attacks Batavia
"Ye Bare & Ye Cubb" is the first play performed in North America in Accomac, Virginia
The Treaty of Nerchinsk is signed by Russia and the Qing Empire
Earl of Mar raises the Jacobite standard at Braemar, Aberdeenshire, starting the Jacobite uprising in Scotland
The British defeat the Americans in the Battle of Long Island
First hydrogen balloon flight (unmanned) reaches an altitude of 900 m
Jacques Necker named as French Minister of Finance
French National Assembly issues the "Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen"
French army and Irish rebels rout a larger British force at the Battle of Castlebar, Ireland
British invasion army lands in North Holland
West India Quay officially opens as London’s first purpose-built dock, featuring the largest brick building in the world
Lord Exmouth bombards Algiers, a refuge for Barbary pirates
Russian Empire defeats the Ottoman Empire at the Battle of Akhaltsikhe in modern-day Georgia
Uruguay gains independence during Brazil-Argentina peace talks
Sauk leaders Black Hawk and Wabokieshiek surrender
First successful oil well drilled near Titusville, Pennsylvania, by Edwin Drake
The first international boat race is held on the River Thames; Oxford beats Harvard
Hurricane hits Florida and the Carolinas, killing about 700
New York City's Metropolitan Opera House, which opened in 1883, catches fire and takes two years to restore
Congress passes the Wilson-Gorman Tariff Act, which includes a graduated income tax later struck down by the Supreme Court
Britain defeats Zanzibar in a 38-minute war (9:02 am - 9:40 am), the shortest recorded war in history

Gabriel Fauré's grand cantata "Prométhée" premieres at Arènes de Béziers, France; performance involved almost 800 singers and musicians (including two wind bands and 15 harps) and an audience of 10,000
Calgary City Rugby Football Club reorganizes as the Tigers
Future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherJack Chesbroplays his final game for the New York Highlanders in a 17-6 loss to the Tigers at Bennett Park, Detroit
Using twenty 137,000 candlepower arc lights, two amateur baseball teams play a night game at White Sox Park
Washington Senator Red Killefer sacrifices a record four times against Detroit
Chicago White Sox pitcherEd Walshno-hits Boston Red Sox 5-0 at White Sox Park
Pyotr Nesterov becomes the first pilot to fly a loop-the-loop in an airplane in Kyiv, Ukraine (new style: September 9, 1913)
Swedish engineer Gideon Sundback of Hoboken applies to patent all-purpose zipper
US war reporter Richard H. Davis visits Leuven in Belgium
Romania declares war on Austria-Hungary
Indians set club record by stealing eight bases in a game
Christy Mathewsonresigns as Cincinnati Reds manager to accept a commission as a captain in the Chemical Warfare Branch of the US Army
Dr. Joseph L. Johnson is named as the US minister to Liberia
American diver Louis Kuehn leads a US medal sweep in the men's 3 m springboard event at the Antwerp Olympics as teammates Clarence Pinkston and Louis Balbach take the minor medals
J.E. Clair of Acme Packing Co. in Green Bay is granted an NFL franchise
The second Women's World Games (track and field competition for women) are conducted over three days at Slottsskogsvallen Stadium in Gothenburg, Sweden
America's oldest aviation school, Parks College, opens
Times Square derailment kills 18 people and injures 100 in New York City Subway's second deadliest accident
200,000 English textile workers strike
International anti-war congress opens in Amsterdam
Brooklyn Dodger Fred Frankhouse no-hits Cincinnati, 5-0, in a 7 2/3 inning game
George E.T. Eyston sets a world auto speed record of 345.49 mph (556 km/h)
New York Yankees pitcher Monte Pearson no-hits the Cleveland Indians, 13-0;Joe DiMaggiohits three triples
Two NYC subway trains collide at 116th Street, killing 2 and injuring 51
Erich Warsitz in a Heinkel He 178 makes the first manned jet-propelled flight with a turbojet engine designed by Hans von Ohain
Nazi Germany demands possession of the Free City of Danzig and the Polish Corridor
Experimental jet plane Caproni-Campini CC-2 makes its maiden flight in Milan
WWII: British Royal Navy captures German U-boat U-570 on its maiden voyage; it is reflagged as HMS Graph and used by the British for nearly two years
Cuba declares war on Germany, Japan, and Italy
200 RAF Halifax bombers attack oil installations in Hamburg
US troops land in Japan after Japan surrenders
102°F (38.89°C) is the highest temperature ever recorded in Cleveland in August
First transmission of a TV program from continental Europe airs on the BBC
General Foods blacklists Jean Muir of The Aldrich Family for alleged communist sympathies
Guinness Book of World Records is first published
Sandy Koufaxfans 14 Reds as both teams combine for a record 23 strikeouts
Curt Roberts of Columbus (International League) hits four home runs in a seven-inning game
Hickory Smoke wins the Hambletonian Stakes
The Constitution of Malaysia comes into force
US performs a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
The US performs a nuclear test over the South Atlantic Ocean as part of Operation Argus to study the Christofilos effect, a theoretical defensive shield to cloud Soviet radar
USSR launches Sputnik 3 with two dogs aboard
Anita Lonsbrough swims a 200 m breaststroke world and Olympic record of 2:49.5
Australian swimmer John Devitt controversially wins the 100 m freestyle gold medal at the Rome Olympics, recording the same time of 55.2 as silver medalist American Lance Larson
British swimmer Anita Lonsbrough sets a 200 m breaststroke world record of 2:49.5, beating the German pair Wiltrud Urselmann and Barbara Göbel to win the gold medal at the Rome Olympics
Francis the Talking Mule is a mystery guest on "What's My Line"
Mariner 2, the first probe to fly by Venus, is launched
The USSR performs a nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya, USSR
The Beatles spend an evening with Elvis Presley at his Los Angeles, California, home
WTVI TV channel 42 in Charlotte, North Carolina (PBS) begins broadcasting
Oakland pitcher Paul Lindblad begins a 385-game consecutive errorless streak
Race riot in Waukegan, Illinois
Naomi Sims is the first Black model on a US cover (Fashion of the Times)

Protest by the Derry Housing Action Committee (DHAC) at the Guildhall's council chamber, after which Eamon Melaugh phones the Northern Ireland Civil Rights Association (NICRA) to organize a march in Derry
Lindy's Pride wins Hambletonian Stakes
Mike Procter hits six consecutive sixes (across two overs)
Operation Lion's Den: US Navy bombards Haiphong Harbor, North Vietnam, from the sea, firing over 700 rounds in 33 minutes
The Grateful Dead holds a benefit concert for the Springfield Creamery (a yogurt company owned by writer Ken Kesey's brother) at the Old Renaissance Faire Grounds in Veneta, Oregon
NY Met Benny Ayala hits a home run in his first at-bat
Veronica and Colin Scargill of England complete their tandem bicycle ride around the world, covering a record 18,020 miles (29,000 km)
Army shoots at market vendor women in Conakry, Guinea
Toby Harrah and Bump Wills hit back-to-back inside-the-park home runs off Yankee Ken Clay at Yankee Stadium as the Rangers win 8-2
Dutch cyclist Gerrie Knetemann wins the UCI Road World Championship, defeating Francesco Moser of Italy and Dane Jorgen Marcussen in Nürburg, West Germany
MLB New York Yankees pitcher Catfish Hunter's 6-2 win gives him a perfect 6-0 record in August
Warrenpoint ambush: 18 British Army soldiers are killed when the Provisional IRA explodes two roadside bombs as a British convoy passes Narrow Water Castle
Chun Doo-hwan is elected president of South Korea
Divers begin to recover a safe found aboard the Italian liner Andrea Doria, which sank on July 26, 1956, but the safe does not yield the rumored jewels
US performs a nuclear test at the Nevada Test Site
Soyuz T-7 returns to Earth
Haiti adopts a constitution
US performs a nuclear test
The USSR performs an underground nuclear test
War Against Indiscipline is launched by the Buhari regime in Nigeria
Space Shuttle Discovery launches its 20th mission, STS-51-I
Protests erupt in Soweto, South Africa, against evictions carried out after an 11-week rent boycott
Dodgers'Tommy Lasordawins his 1,000th game as manager, topping Philadelphia 4-2
100 people march through Bensonhurst protesting racial violence
Chris Patton wins the 89th US Golf Amateur Championship
52 Americans arrive in Turkey from Iraq
Brewers-Blue Jays game is delayed 35 minutes due to gnats
Market prices plunge as OPEC nears an informal agreement to increase output to cover the shortfall due to the invasion; cash market trading experiences an abrupt decline
Rosa Motawins the women's marathon (2:31:27) at the European Championships in Split, Croatia
Moldova declares independence from the Soviet Union
CFL revokes BC Lions' franchise
Mets tradeDavid Coneto Toronto for Jeff Kent and Ryan Thompson
The Rainbow Bridge connecting Tokyo's Shibaura and the island of Odaiba is completed
Yak-40 crashes in Tajikistan, killing 79 people with 1 survivor
Worst fire in New York in 80 years ends after 4 days
Dino Ciccarelliis traded by the Detroit Red Wings to the Tampa Bay Lightning
Mars makes its closest approach to Earth in nearly 60,000 years, passing within 34,646,418 miles (55,758,005 km)
Australia wins its first-ever Olympic men's field hockey gold medal with a 2-1 win after extra time against the Netherlands in Athens
China's first-ever gold in Olympic Games men's track and field; Liu Xiang wins the 110 m hurdles in Athens, equaling Colin Jackson's 1993 world record time of 12.91 seconds
Craig Kilborn's final appearance as host of "The Late Late Show" on CBS after 1,190 episodes

German kayaker Birgit Fischer wins gold in K-4 500 m and silver in K-2 500 m in Athens; she is the first woman in any sport to win gold medals at six different Olympics, with golds 24 years apart, and the first person to win two or more medals in five different Games
In a huge upset in the men's basketball semifinal at the Athens Olympics, Argentina beats a star-studded but unsettled US team 89-81; they go on to take gold and the US takes bronze
Osleidys Menéndez of Cuba throws an Olympic record of 71.53 m to easily beat German Steffi Nerius for the women's javelin gold medal at the Athens Games
Timothy Mack vaults an Olympic record of 5.95 m to win the pole vault gold medal over fellow American Toby Stevenson (5.90 m) at the Athens Olympics
Comair Flight 5191 crashes on takeoff from Blue Grass Airport in Lexington, Kentucky, bound for Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport in Atlanta, Georgia, killing 49 of the 50 on board

Australia claims the final Tri Nations Rugby Series with a 25-20 win over New Zealand in Brisbane; Argentina's Pumas join the competition the following year; the series is rebranded as The Rugby Championship
First interplanetary human voice recording broadcast from Mars Rover Curiosity
Dove Cameronreleases a cover of "On Top of the World" by Imagine Dragons
Medical charity Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) calls the international response to Ebola “irresponsible” and “slow and derisory”
Greek President Prokopis Pavlopoulos names top Supreme Court judge Vassiliki Thanou as caretaker Prime Minister, Greece's first female Prime Minister
Former Spice GirlMel Bannounces she is entering rehab for alcohol and sex addiction following her acrimonious divorce
UN releases report saying Myanmar military leaders should face genocide and crimes against humanity charges for violence against Rohingya
At least 25 people killed in a fire at a bar, deliberately set by gang members in Coatzacoalcos, Mexico
Race car driver Jessi Combs dies while setting a new fastest women's land speed record of 522.783 mph (841.34 km/h) at Alvord Desert, Oregon (posthumously awarded in 2020)
Australian terrorist Brenton Tarrant is sentenced to life without parole for the killing of 51 mosque worshippers in Christchurch, New Zealand, the first time the country imposes the sentence
Hurricane Laura makes landfall in Louisiana near the Texas border as a Category 4 storm with 150 mph winds, killing at least 16 people
Britain's Prince Andrew is served with a US federal lawsuit alleging he sexually abused a teenager 20 years ago
GymnastSimone Biles(26) wins her record 8th US Championship in San Jose, California
Tour Championship, Men's Golf, East Lake GC, GA: Norwegian Viktor Hovland secures a five-stroke win over AmericanXander Schauffelefor the biggest victory of his career
Jason Kelce and his brother Travis Kelce sign a deal for their “New Heights” podcast with Amazon's Wondery for more than $100 million
Record 10-day streak of 35 degrees Celsius or above in Tokyo, Japan, longest consecutive streak since 1875
Holy Roman EmperorCharles Vabdicates in favor of his brother Ferdinand I
Battle of the Combahee River near Beaufort, South Carolina: American abolitionistJohn Laurensis killed while leading the charge
Battle of Dresden:Napoleondefeats the Austrians
Battle of Bergendal: General Buller defeats the Boers under GeneralLouis Botha
WWI: Germans bombard Usdau on the second day of the Battle ofTannenberg
Paavo Nurmiruns a world record in the 3000 m (8:28.6)
Kellogg-BriandPact, 60 nations agree to condemn the "recourse to war for the solution of international controversies"
Shah of IranReza Shah Pahlaviabdicates throne in favor of his sonMohammad Reza Pahlavi
Soviet woman sniperLyudmila Pavlichenkoarrives in Washington D.C., the first Soviet citizen welcomed at the White House byEleanor Roosevelt
Having already won 5,000 m and 10,000 m gold medals, Czech starEmil Zátopekclaims a rare Olympic treble by winning the Helsinki Games marathon in an Olympic Record time of 2:23:03.2
Romantic comedy film "Roman Holiday," starringAudrey Hepburn,Gregory Peck, andEddie Albert, is released
Washington Senators ownerClark Griffithsays he will probably accept an offer to move the team to Minnesota

Walt Disney's musical film "Mary Poppins," directed by Robert Stevenson and starringJulie AndrewsandDick Van Dyke, with songs by Richard and Robert Sherman, premieres at Grauman's Chinese Theatre in Los Angeles, California, and wins five Academy Awards
British sailorFrancis Chichesterbegins the first solo sail around the world, leaving Plymouth aboard the Gipsy Moth IV and completing it in May 1967
Jimi Hendrixcreates his last studio recording at Electric Lady Studios in NYC, an instrumental called "Slow Blues"
MLB Cincinnati Reds'Joe Morganis the first to hit 200 home runs and have 500 stolen bases

Lord Mountbatten, 1st EarlMountbattenof Burma, cousin of Queen Elizabeth II and last Viceroy of India, is killed along with three companions, two of whom are children, by the IRA when his boat is blown up near Sligo, Ireland
MLB Oakland A's outfielderRickey Hendersonsteals his 119th base of the season, breakingLou Brock's record
US PresidentRonald Reaganannounces the "Teacher in Space" project
GeneralIbrahim Babangidaoverthrows Major GeneralMuhammadu Buhari's regime in Nigeria
"No Fences," the second studio album byGarth Brooks, is released (Billboard Album of the Year 1991)
Nigerian PresidentIbrahim Babangidacreates nine states: Abia, Enugu, Delta, Jigawa, Kebbi, Osun, Kogi, Taraba, and Yobe
Angelina Jolieis named a UNHCR Goodwill Ambassador at UNHCR headquarters in Geneva
Hong Kong martial arts film "Hero," starringJet Li, opens in the US and becomes the first Chinese-language film to reach #1 at the US box office
58th Emmy Awards: 24, The Office,Kiefer Sutherland, andMariska Hargitaywin
US SenatorBarack Obamabecomes the first African American to be nominated by a major political party for President of the United States, by the Democratic Party
"Thrift Shop" single is released byMacklemore& Ryan Lewis (Billboard Song of the Year 2013, Grammy Award for Best Rap Performance, Best Rap Song 2014)
"The Shepherd's Crown" byTerry Pratchettis published by Doubleday five months after the author's death as the last book in the Discworld series
Former five-weight world boxing championFloyd Mayweather Jr.defeats MMA fighterConor McGregorin his debut match in the 10th round in Las Vegas
Mike Penceaccepts the nomination for vice president at the Republican convention, calling for "law and order" after the shooting ofJacob Blake
British bandOasisannounce a reunion tour of Britain and Ireland, 16 years after their acrimonious split
Krakatoa volcano, located west of Java in Indonesia, erupts with a force of 200 megatons of TNT, killing approximately 36,000 people
Spanish flu arrives in Boston, beginning the second and deadliest wave in the US
The 540-meter (1,772 ft) tall Ostankino Tower in Moscow catches fire, resulting in the deaths of three people
Ashikaga Yoshikazu, 5th Japanese shōgun of the Ashikaga shogunate (1423-5), born in Japan (d. 1425)
George the Bearded, Duke of Saxony (1500-39), born in Meissen, Germany (d. 1539)
Anna of Brandenburg, Queen of Denmark, born in Berlin (d. 1514)
Alexander Farnese, Italian nobleman, mercenary, Governor of Spanish Netherlands (1578-92), and Duke of Parma (1586-92), born in Rome. Papal States (d. 1592)
Hans Leo Hassler, German composer, born in the Free Imperial City Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1612)
Simon Besler, German composer, born in Brieg, Silesia (d. 1633)
Maria van Oosterwijck, Dutch flower painter, born in Nootdorp, Netherlands (d. 1693)
Charles Calvert, 3rd Baron Baltimore, Governor of the Province of Maryland, born in Salisbury, England (d. 1715)
Johann Samuel Welter, German composer, born in Obersontheim, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1720)
John Hervey, 1st Earl of Bristol, English politician, born in Bury St Edmunds, Suffolk (d. 1751)
Ivan V, co-tsar of Russia (1682-89), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1696)
Anne Marie of Orléans, Queen of Italy, born in Château de Saint-Cloud, France (d. 1728)
Otto Ferdinand von Abensberg und Traun, Austrian field marshal, born in Ödenburg, Austria (d. 1748)
Baal Shem Tov, Jewish mystical rabbi (founder of the Hasidic Judaism), born in Okopy Świętej Trójcy, Kingdom of Poland (d. 1760)
Jan Nepveu, Dutch governor of Suriname (1768-79), born in Amsterdam (d. 1779)
John Joachim Zubly, Swiss-American Continental Congressman, born in St. Gall, Switzerland (d. 1781)
Johann Georg Hamann, German counter-enlightenment philosopher ("Reason is language"), born in Königsberg, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1788)
Michel Delalande, French sacred music composer and maître de musique (Cathedral of Chartres, 1761-85), born in La Flèche, France (d. 1812)
Herman Muntinghe, Dutch theologian and writer (History of Mankind), born in Termunten, Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1824)
Anne François Mellinet, French-Belgian general (Maastricht), born in Corbeil-Essonnes, France (d. 1852)
Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel, German philosopher and inventor (dialectic), born in Stuttgart, Württemberg (d. 1831)
Athanase Laurent Charles Coquerel, Protestant theologian and president of the Presbyterian Council of Paris (L'Orthodoxie Moderne / Modern Orthodoxy), born in Paris, France (d. 1868)[1]
Edward Beecher, American theologian, born in East Hampton, New York (d. 1895)
Hannibal Hamlin, American attorney and 15th Vice President of U.S. (1861-65), born in Paris, Massachusetts (d. 1891)
Hiram Gregory Berry, American politician and Major General (Union Army), born in Thomaston, Main (d. 1863)
Frank Stillman Nickerson, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Swanville, Maine (d. 1917)
Hermann Kipper, German music teacher and composer, born in Koblenz, Rhenish Prussia (d. 1910)
James A. Walker, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Augusta County, Virginia (d. 1901)
Albijn van de Abeele, Flemish author and painter, born in Sint-Martens-Latem, Belgium (d. 1918)
Thomas Burberry, English fashion designer, founder of luxury brand Burberry, and inventor of gabardine, born in Surrey, England (d. 1926)
Heinrich Urban, German violinist, composer, and teacher of Wanda Landowska and Ignace Jan Paderewski, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1901)
Emory Upton, Bvt Major General (Union Army), born in Batavia, New York (d. 1881)
Gustave J Waffelaert, Flemish theologist and bishop of Bridge, born in Rollegem, Belgium (d. 1931)
Manuel Acuña, Mexican poet (Nocturno), born in Saltillo, Coahuila, Mexic0 (d. 1873)
Iwan Franko, Ukrainian writer and political activist, born in Nahuievychi, Galicia (d. 1916)
Giuseppe Peano, Italian mathematician and glottologist, born in Spinetta, Piedmont, Italy (d. 1932)
Charles G. Dawes, 30th Vice President of the United States, banker and 1925 Nobel Peace Prize winner, born in Marietta, Ohio (d. 1951)
James Henry Breasted, American Egyptologist and anthropologist, born in Rockford, Illinois (d. 1935)
Karl Haushofer, German soldier and geographer, born in Munich, Bavaria (d. 1946)
Amado Nervo [Juan Crisóstomo Ruiz de Nervo], Mexican writer and poet, born in Tepic, Nayarit, Mexico (d. 1919)
Carl Bosch, German chemist (BASF, Nobel 1931), born in Cologne, German Empire (d. 1940)
Katharine McCormick, American biologist, suffragist and philanthropist who funded research for the first birth control pill, born in Dexter, Michigan (d. 1967)
Ernst Wetter, Member of the Swiss Federal Council, born in Töss, Winterthur, Switzerland (d. 1963)
Lloyd C. Douglas, American novelist (The Big Fisherman), born in Columbia City, Indiana (d. 1951)
Joseph John Richards, American composer, born in Cwmafan, Wales (d. 1956)
Jaroslav Křička, Czech composer, born in Kelč, Moravia, Austrian Empire (d. 1969)
Harry Antrim, American actor (Miracle on 34th St, Devil's Doorway), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1967)
Vincent Auriol, President of France (1947-53), born in Revel, France (d. 1966)
Eric Coates, English viola player and composer, born in Hucknall, Nottinghamshire, England (d. 1957)
Rebecca Clarke, British-American violist and classical composer (Morpheus), born in Harrow, England (d. 1979)
James Finlayson, Scottish-American stage and silent and "talkie" film actor (Laurel and Hardy films), born in Larbert, Stirlingshire, Scotland (d. 1953)
Man Ray, American artist and photographer (Dada), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1976)
Charles Meredith, American actor (Court of Last Resort), born in Knoxville, Pennsylvania (d. 1964)
Faina Ranevskaya [Faina Georgievna Feldman], Russian actress, born in Taganrog, Don Voisko Oblast, Russian Empire (d. 1984)
Leon Theremin, Russian-Soviet electronic musical instruments inventor, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1993)
Ad van Emmenes, Dutch soccer reporter, born in Amsterdam (d. 1989)
Byron Foulger, American actor (River's Edge, Up in Smoke), born in Ogden, Utah (d. 1970)
C. S. Forester, English historical novelist (Horatio Hornblower), born in Cairo, Egypt (d. 1966)
Shalva Mikhailovich Taktakishvili, Russian composer, teacher, and conductor (Georgian State Orchestra, 1952-65), born in Kvemo-Khviti, Georgia (d. 1965)
Al Ritz [Albert Joachim], American vaudevillian (The Ritz Brothers), dancer and actor (The Gorilla;, Hi'ya, Chum; Straight, Place and Show), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1965)
Roger Pryor, American actor (Lady by Choice, Belle of the Nineties, Identity Unknown), born in New York City (d. 1974)
Herbert Menges, English composer, born in Hove, East Sussex, England (d. 1972)
Xavier Villaurrutia, Mexican poet (Nocturno de los Angeles), born in Mexico City (d. 1950)
Norah Lofts, British author (The Town House), born in Shipdham, Norfolk, England (d. 1983)
Frederick O'Neal, American actor, theatre producer and television director (Pinky, Car 54 Where Are You), born in Brooksville, Mississippi (d. 1992)
Frank Leahy, American football coach (Notre Dame), born in O'Neill, Nebraska (d. 1973)
Kurt Wegner, German artist, born in Köln, Germany (d. 1985)
Lester Young, American jazz saxophonist, born in Woodville, Mississippi (d. 1959)
Sidney R. Yates, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Illinois), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2000)
Sylvère Maes, Belgian cyclist (Tour de France 1936, 39), born in Gistel, Belgium (d. 1966)
Johnny Eck [Eckhardt], American sideshow performer, actor (Freaks; Tarzan films), magician, and artist, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1991)
Kathleen "Kay" Walsh, British actress (The Horse's Mouth, Oliver Twist), born in Chelsea, London, England (d. 2005)
Patience Edney, British nurse and communist, born in Orpington, Kent, England (d. 1996)
Peter Gretton, British vice admiral, born in Franham, Surrey (d. 1992)
Arthur Benfield, British police detective (Head of Cheshire CID), born in Ashton Upon Mersey, England (d. 1995)
Donald M. MacKinnon, Scottish philosopher, born in Oban, Scotland (d. 1994)
Catherine Marshall, American editor and writer, born in Johnson City, Tennessee (d. 1983)
Walter Heller, economist (Old Myths & New Realities), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1987)
Martha Raye [Margaret Reed], American actress (Waikiki Wedding, Martha Raye Show), born in Butte, Montana (d. 1994)
Tony Harris, South African cricket batsman (3 Tests, 1 x 50; Transvaal) and rugby union five-eighth (5 Tests), born in Kimberley, South Africa (d. 1993)
Peanuts Lowrey, American baseball player, born in Culver City, California (d. 1986)
Jan Cox, Belgian-American Expressionist painter, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1980)
John Donkin Dormand, Baron Dormand of Easington, British politician (Labour Party), born in Haswell, County Durham, England (d. 2003)
James Molyneaux [Baron Molyneaux of Killead], MP (Ulster unionist), born in Killead, Ireland (d. 2015)
Georg Alexander, Duke of Mecklenburg, head of the House of Mecklenburg-Strelitz, born in Nice, France (d. 1996)
Leo Penn, American actor and film director, born in Lawrence, Massachusetts (d. 1998)
David Rowbotham, Australian poet and journalist, born in Toowoomba, Queensland, Australia (d. 2010)
Darry Cowl, French musician and actor, born in Vittel, France (d. 2006)
Richard Rutt, English bishop of Leicester, born in Lengford, England (d. 2011)
Tony Crombie, English jazz drummer and bandleader, born in Bishopsgate, London (d. 1999)
Kristen Nygaard, Norwegian computer scientist, born in Oslo, Norway (d. 2002)
Patricia "Pat" Coombs, British comedienne and actress (Carry On Again Doctor, Cranford), born in Camberwell, London (d. 2002)
Hugh Byatt, British diplomat, born in Edinburgh (d. 2011)
Liselott Linsenhoff, German equestrian rider (Olympic gold 1968, 72), born in Frankfurt, Germany (d. 1999)
Norma Connolly, American actress (Ruby Anderson-General Hospital, The Young Marrieds), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1998)
Jack Thompson, British politician (Labour), born in Ashington, England (d. 2011)
Ján Zachara, Slovak boxer (Olympic gold Czechoslovakia featherweight 1952), born in Trenčín, Slovakia (d. 2025)
Joan Kroc [Mansfield], American 3rd wife of Ray Kroc, the owner of McDonald's and the San Diego Padres, born in West St. Paul, Minnesota (d. 2003)
Mangosuthu Buthelezi, South African politician (Chief Minister of KwaZulu 1972-94), born in Mahlabathini, Natal, South Africa (d. 2023)[1]
Othmar Schneider, Austrian alpine skier (Olympic gold men's slalom, silver downhill 1952), born in Lech am Arlberg, Austria (d. 2012)
Peter Richard Tahourdin, English-Australian composer, born in Bramdean, Hampshire (d. 2009)
John Daly, British trade union leader (National and Local Government Officers' Association (NALGO)), born in Clerkenwell, England (d. 1999)
John Watts, British army and Chief of Defense (Omani Armed Forces), born in Portsmouth, England (d. 2003)
Joe Cunningham, American baseball utility (MLB All Star 1959, 59²; St. Louis Cardinals, Chicago White Sox, Washington Senators), born in Paterson, New Jersey (d. 2021)
Sri Chinmoy, Indian guru, born in Shakpura, British India (now Bangladesh) (d. 2007)
Sven Tumba, Swedish ice hockey center, football winger, golfer, course designer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 2011)
Antonia Fraser, British author and biographer (Mary Queen of Scots), born in London, England
François Glorieux, Flemish pianist and composer, born in Courtrai, Belgium (d. 2023)[1]
Joke Smit, Dutch writer (Het onbehagen bij de vrouw / The Discontent of Women), and feminist leader, born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1981)
Eddie Connachan, Scottish soccer goalkeeper (2 caps; Dunfermline Athletic, Middlesbrough, Falkirk), born in Prestonpans, Scotland (d. 2021)
Ernie Broglio, American MLB baseball pitcher, 1959-66 (St. Louis Cardinals, Chicago Cubs), born in Berkeley, California (d. 2019)
Frank Yablans, American screenwriter (North Dallas Forty), and film studio executive (Paramount), born in New York City (d. 2014)
